# Questions in Livestream 8

Live Stream #8: Questions behind the Eight Ball

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction |
|---:|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AGAwu3NDnCM?t=283" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:43</a> | How many intact or partially intact royal tombs have been found, including Tutankhamun, Psusennes I, and King Hor of Dynasty 13? | If "royal" includes non-kings, there are several; for kings, the main examples are Tutankhamun, the Tanite kings Psusennes I, Amenemope, Shoshenq II, and the partly intact King Hor, with the Tanite burials among the most intact. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AGAwu3NDnCM?t=681" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">11:21</a> | How do scholars explain the mass departure from Avaris if they reject the biblical narrative? | The common explanation is that the Nile branch shifted and the water supply moved, but he thinks that does not fit well because the port remained active and the silting fits later Pi-Ramesses better. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AGAwu3NDnCM?t=869" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:29</a> | What are your thoughts on theistic evolution from people who teach that Genesis is poetic instead of historical from an ancient Near Eastern perspective? | He is cautious and still developing his view; he resists tying Genesis to changing scientific models and stresses that poetic form does not exclude historical content. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AGAwu3NDnCM?t=1094" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">18:14</a> | Is the NET Bible right that the sons of Heth in Abraham's story have no known connection with the Hittites? | He is skeptical of that note; Hittites existed well before the thirteenth century BC, and he thinks they could fit the time of Abraham even if early evidence is limited. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AGAwu3NDnCM?t=1278" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">21:18</a> | Is Jeush from Genesis 36 mentioned in the Story of Sinuhe, as Steve Rudd claims? | He rejects the claim, saying the chronology is off by roughly two centuries and that the biblical and Sinuhe timelines do not align. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AGAwu3NDnCM?t=1525" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">25:25</a> | How tall were the pharaohs, and what did ancient Egyptians use for toilets and soap? | Pharaohs were generally around five to six feet tall, with Tutankhamun perhaps near 5'2"; Egyptians used simple indoor toilet arrangements with pots, and had a rudimentary soap from oil and natron. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AGAwu3NDnCM?t=1694" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:14</a> | Should Exodus 6:3 be translated as a question, as Michael Heiser argues? | He thinks the move is problematic because the Hebrew lacks clear question markers and turning declarative statements into questions can too easily negate the text. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AGAwu3NDnCM?t=1991" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:11</a> | Where do you think the Tower of Babel was located? | Probably somewhere in southern Mesopotamia, perhaps Sumer or Akkad; he sees it as a ziggurat-like alternative worship platform rather than a tower into outer space. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AGAwu3NDnCM?t=2144" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">35:44</a> | What cultural insight explains why honey was not allowed in cereal offerings? | Honey could be clean for ordinary food but not holy for offerings, partly because raw honey might contain insect parts and because offering standards differ from ordinary food purity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AGAwu3NDnCM?t=2393" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">39:53</a> | Do you have any personal favorite ancient Egyptian texts or inscriptions? | He names Thutmose III's annals, wisdom texts such as Amenemope, Ipuwer, Wenamun, Sinuhe, and some tomb robbery papyri. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AGAwu3NDnCM?t=2484" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">41:24</a> | Do you see Genesis 1 as a response to Enuma Elish? | Yes, but not only Enuma Elish; he sees Genesis 1 as a polemic or alternative myth addressing many ancient Near Eastern creation traditions for Israel's mixed community. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AGAwu3NDnCM?t=2689" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">44:49</a> | Why did pyramid construction end? | It became too expensive; over time pyramids shrank, used cheaper materials, and eventually gave way to rock-cut tombs with small pyramid symbols. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AGAwu3NDnCM?t=2892" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">48:12</a> | Does Genesis 1 admit second, third, or fourth readings, and how does it correlate with other creation stories? | He says Genesis 1 is complex and can be read in its ancient Near Eastern context, especially as polemic, but multiple readings depend on method and not every reading is valid. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AGAwu3NDnCM?t=3096" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">51:36</a> | Is there a book or online course you recommend for learning ancient Egyptian? | He recommends Collier and Manley's basic book, his own introductory uniliteral videos, James Hoch's Middle Egyptian textbook for more advanced work, and eventually a reference grammar. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AGAwu3NDnCM?t=3199" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">53:19</a> | What tahash animal was used for tent coverings? | He thinks the question may refer to "porpoise skins," but says the term is debated and could refer to an aquatic or terrestrial animal; he would need more research to answer definitively. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AGAwu3NDnCM?t=3379" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">56:19</a> | Are there resources on ancient Egypt from an economic perspective? | Yes; he recommends Jack J. Janssen's Commodity Prices from the Ramessid Period as a major starting point for Egyptian economics. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AGAwu3NDnCM?t=3490" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">58:10</a> | Are there cultural references from the patriarchs or judges that are lost on us but support historicity? | He points to Abraham's 318 trained men, comparing small military numbers in the Amarna letters and martial training scenes at Beni Hasan as plausible cultural context. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AGAwu3NDnCM?t=3748" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:02:28</a> | Do predestination debates on Romans 9 about Pharaoh ignore the Egyptological context of the Hebrew hardening verbs hezek and kabad? | He does not think they necessarily ignore it; the verbs have semantic ranges, and a particular reading may be valid without exhausting all possible meanings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AGAwu3NDnCM?t=3921" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:05:21</a> | Why are scholars quick to accept the Sea Peoples or Philistine conquest and settlement in Canaan, but not the Hebrew account? Is there more evidence for the Philistines? | There is somewhat more Egyptian evidence for Philistines because they interacted with Egypt on the coastal route, but he argues similar circumstances could support Israel in the foothills and that scholarly bias plays a role. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AGAwu3NDnCM?t=4214" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:10:14</a> | What do you think about the Noah story, geological evidence, and Ararat or Urartu? | He says he is not ready to answer fully; he notes Ararat and Urartu are linguistically related and says he has problems with some local-flood arguments but needs more study. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AGAwu3NDnCM?t=4392" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:13:12</a> | What can you tell us about the stela of Ankh-ef-en-Khonsu, and how often do Egyptologists get pestered about it? | He is not familiar enough with it to answer on the fly; after hearing the Crowley/Thelema context, he says it interests him and he would need to look it up and translate it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AGAwu3NDnCM?t=4498" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:58</a> | When and why was Moses not allowed to enter the promised land: after the army refused to attack Canaan, or after the striking of the rock? | He says the rock episode disqualified Moses, because Moses added his own anger to God's message, while the earlier refusal disqualified the people. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AGAwu3NDnCM?t=4750" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:19:10</a> | Since Egyptian reliefs mix history, artistic rules, religion, and symbolism, is this an example of the ancient Near East relating history through mythology? | Yes, but he frames it as reception history: Egyptians related events through religious and afterlife concerns, not as detached history for later readers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AGAwu3NDnCM?t=4976" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:22:56</a> | Would you make a video on the Theban triad, and do you have recommendations? | He thinks a video on Egyptian gods and goddesses, including the Theban triad, is a worthwhile idea and says he will add it to his ideas file. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AGAwu3NDnCM?t=5106" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:25:06</a> | Is restoring dinosaurs like Jurassic Park a valid science question, and would ancient Egyptian life be applicable in our context? | He calls dinosaur restoration an engineering question, then says ancient Egypt differs greatly from modern society, but still teaches us about stable features of human nature. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/AGAwu3NDnCM?t=5553" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:32:33</a> | What does the Egyptian language tell us about the development of the Goa'uld on P3X-888? | Almost nothing; he treats Stargate as fun science fiction, not real Egyptology, and jokes that the movie's hieroglyph translations are also poor. |
